scenario_results.reason: The lane moved a routine exhibit-promotion campaign from accepted to completed only after creative approvals, ticketing offer records, delivery handoffs, and go-live proof aligned.
Missing price language, disclaimer text, channel spec, and promo-code evidence produced a blocked state and prerequisite request rather than speculative execution.
A request to change price presentation and promise refunds was refused and routed to pricing, revenue, and guest-service owners instead of being executed in-lane.
Conflicting CMS, ticketing, and proof records triggered rework handling and prevented unsupported closure.
Suspicious confirmations, payment complaints, and venue-security restrictions stopped ordinary creative execution and generated the required escalation package.
The lane refused to mark work complete when launch proof, publish logs, and publisher acceptance records were absent.

Arts-and-entertainment creative terminology such as key art, trafficking sheet, Ad-ID, presale window, and digital-signage rotation was preserved rather than flattened into generic marketing language.
Guest-service and front-office decisions on expired tickets and service recovery were rejected cleanly and routed to the correct owners.
Missing facility context, offer rules, approval owners, and system retrieval hooks triggered low-confidence escalation instead of confident synthesis.
FTC price-disclosure guidance conflicting with a tenant playbook was recorded as a policy conflict requiring escalation before approval.
Peak on-sale demand was handled as a high-volume execution queue with explicit deliverables, owners, and blockers but without assuming pricing or guest-service authority.
Tenant adaptation preserved the public baseline while separating workflows by brand, business unit, approval chain, and delivery system.

notes: All 12 defined scenarios are represented in functionality-map.json scenario_coverage.
All required shared, execution-processing-and-fulfillment, and retail-consumer-and-hospitality abilities are mapped and validated.
All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold with complete ability coverage for the execution-processing-and-fulfillment and retail-consumer-and-hospitality contract.
The package stays inside arts, entertainment, and recreation creative operations and does not claim pricing, refund, fraud, accessibility-policy, safety, or legal approval authority.
